questions.q: A symplectic form $\omega$ on $M$ is a 2-form satisfying which two conditions?
questions.explain: By definition $\omega$ is symplectic when $d\omega=0$ (closed) and $\omega_p$ is non-degenerate at every $p$ (the induced map $T_pM\to T_p^*M$, $v\mapsto \iota_v\omega$, is an isomorphism). Positive definiteness is the wrong condition — $\omega$ is alternating, not symmetric.

questions.q: Why must a symplectic manifold have even dimension?
choices: Closed forms only exist in even dimensions.
An alternating non-degenerate bilinear form on $\mathbb{R}^d$ exists only when $d$ is even.
Stokes's theorem fails in odd dimensions.
The Lie bracket vanishes in odd dimensions.
questions.hint: Think about the determinant of an antisymmetric matrix in odd size.
questions.explain: An antisymmetric $d\times d$ matrix $A$ satisfies $\det A = (-1)^d\det A$, so $\det A = 0$ when $d$ is odd. Non-degeneracy of $\omega_p$ (an antisymmetric bilinear form on $T_pM$) is impossible in odd dimension.

questions.q: On a symplectic $2n$-manifold, the top exterior power $\omega^n = \omega\wedge\cdots\wedge\omega$ is...
choices: a nowhere-vanishing volume form, so $M$ is canonically oriented
questions.hint: Non-degeneracy of $\omega$ is equivalent to $\omega^n\ne 0$ pointwise.
questions.explain: Non-degeneracy of $\omega$ at $p$ is equivalent to $\omega_p^n\ne 0\in\Lambda^{2n}T_p^*M$. So $\omega^n$ is a nowhere-vanishing top form — a volume form — and it gives $M$ a canonical orientation. (When $M$ is closed, $\omega$ cannot itself be exact: if $\omega=d\alpha$ then $\omega^n=d(\alpha\wedge\omega^{n-1})$ would integrate to $0$.)

questions.q: Darboux's theorem states that around every point of $(M^{2n},\omega)$ there is a chart in which $\omega$ equals which canonical form?
questions.explain: Darboux: there exist local coordinates $(p_1,\dots,p_n,q_1,\dots,q_n)$ in which $\omega = \sum_{i=1}^n dp_i\wedge dq_i$. The form $\sum p_i\,dq_i$ is the Liouville/tautological 1-form whose exterior derivative is the canonical $\omega$ on $T^*N$.

questions.q: What is the most striking pedagogical consequence of Darboux's theorem?
choices: Symplectic manifolds are classified by their dimension.
There are no local invariants — all symplectic manifolds of dimension $2n$ are locally the same.
Symplectic manifolds admit a Riemannian metric uniquely.
questions.hint: Compare with the Riemannian case (curvature) or the Kähler case (complex structure).
questions.explain: Darboux says there is no symplectic analogue of curvature: locally every $(M^{2n},\omega)$ looks like $(\mathbb{R}^{2n},\omega_0)$. All symplectic invariants are global. Dimension alone does not classify globally — Gromov capacities, symplectic homology, and Floer-theoretic invariants distinguish symplectic manifolds with the same dimension.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a corollary of Darboux's theorem?
choices: Every symplectic manifold of dimension $2$ is locally area-preserving-equivalent to $(\mathbb{R}^2, dx\wedge dy)$.
Every symplectic chart can be chosen so that $\omega = \sum dp_i\wedge dq_i$ on its domain.
Every closed symplectic manifold is symplectomorphic to a torus.
There is no local symplectic curvature invariant.
questions.hint: Darboux is local; classification of closed symplectic manifolds is wide open.
questions.explain: Darboux gives local equivalence only. Globally, classifying closed symplectic manifolds is a hard, open problem (think Kähler vs non-Kähler, Gromov non-squeezing, exotic symplectic structures on $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$). Tori are very far from being the only example.

questions.q: Given $H\colon M\to\mathbb{R}$, the Hamiltonian vector field $X_H$ is defined by which equation?
questions.hint: Use non-degeneracy of $\omega$ to invert the differential of $H$.
questions.explain: The defining identity is $\iota_{X_H}\omega = dH$. Non-degeneracy of $\omega$ means the bundle map $TM\to T^*M$, $v\mapsto\iota_v\omega$, is an isomorphism, so $dH$ has a unique pre-image $X_H$. The gradient $\nabla H$ requires a metric, not a symplectic form.

questions.q: Why is the energy $H$ conserved along the flow of $X_H$?
questions.hint: Use the defining identity $\iota_{X_H}\omega = dH$ and that $\omega$ is alternating.
questions.explain: $\dot H = X_H(H) = dH(X_H) = (\iota_{X_H}\omega)(X_H) = \omega(X_H,X_H) = 0$ since $\omega$ is alternating. Energy conservation is automatic — built into the geometry, not an extra hypothesis.

questions.q: The flow of $X_H$ preserves the symplectic form $\omega$ because Cartan's magic formula gives $\mathcal{L}_{X_H}\omega = $...
questions.explain: Cartan's formula gives $\mathcal{L}_{X_H}\omega = d(\iota_{X_H}\omega) + \iota_{X_H}d\omega = d(dH) + \iota_{X_H}\cdot 0 = 0+0 = 0$. So the flow $\Phi_t^{X_H}$ is a symplectomorphism for every $t$ — Liouville's theorem in geometric form.

questions.q: On a symplectic manifold the Poisson bracket of $f,g\in C^\infty(M)$ is defined as...
questions.hint: Plug the two Hamiltonian vector fields into $\omega$.
questions.explain: By definition $\{f,g\} = \omega(X_f,X_g) = X_f(g) = -X_g(f)$. The first equality is the definition; the others use $\iota_{X_f}\omega = df$. The bracket of ordinary functions is identically zero — they need to come from $\omega$ to interact.

questions.q: Which structural property does $(C^\infty(M),\{\cdot,\cdot\})$ NOT satisfy automatically?
questions.hint: It's a Lie bracket, not a commutative product.
questions.explain: Antisymmetry, Jacobi, and Leibniz hold (the first two make it a Lie algebra; Leibniz makes it a Poisson algebra). Commutativity fails — the whole point of the bracket is to be antisymmetric, the opposite of commutative.

questions.q: Quantisation replaces the Poisson bracket of classical observables with which quantum object?
choices: The product of operators $\hat A\hat B$
The commutator divided by $i\hbar$, $[\hat A,\hat B]/(i\hbar)$
questions.explain: The Dirac correspondence: $\{f,g\}\leadsto [\hat A,\hat B]/(i\hbar)$ where $\hat A,\hat B$ are the operators quantising $f,g$. To leading order in $\hbar$ the quantum commutator reproduces the classical bracket — that is what makes geometric quantisation a quantisation.

questions.q: A submanifold $L$ of a symplectic $(M^{2n},\omega)$ is Lagrangian when which two conditions hold?
questions.hint: Half the dimension of $M$, and $\omega$ vanishes on tangent vectors of $L$.
questions.explain: A Lagrangian is a maximal isotropic submanifold: $\omega$ vanishes on $TL\times TL$ ("isotropic") and $\dim L = \frac12\dim M = n$ ("maximal"). Smaller-than-$n$ isotropics exist but are not Lagrangian.

questions.q: On the cotangent bundle $T^*N$ with its canonical $\omega = -d\lambda$, the zero section $\{(x,0):x\in N\}$ is...
questions.hint: Compute $\dim$ and check $\omega$ on tangent vectors of the zero section.
questions.explain: The zero section has dimension $\dim N = n = \frac12\dim T^*N$, and its tangent vectors all have momentum coordinate zero, so $\omega = \sum dp_i\wedge dq_i$ vanishes on them. More generally the graph of any closed 1-form $\alpha\in\Omega^1(N)$ is a Lagrangian in $T^*N$.

questions.q: A canonical relation between $(M_1,\omega_1)$ and $(M_2,\omega_2)$ is a Lagrangian submanifold of which symplectic manifold?
choices: $(M_1\times M_2,\omega_1\ominus\omega_2)$ — i.e. $\omega_1$ on the first factor minus $\omega_2$ on the second
questions.hint: Graphs of symplectomorphisms are the prototype — try $\omega_1 - f^*\omega_2$.
questions.explain: The product $(M_1\times M_2,\omega_1\ominus\omega_2 := \pi_1^*\omega_1 - \pi_2^*\omega_2)$ is the right symplectic structure: the graph of a symplectomorphism $f\colon M_1\to M_2$ is then a Lagrangian. Canonical relations compose Lagrangians and turn $\mathbf{Symp}$ into a symplectic category.

questions.q: Hamiltonian Floer homology applies Morse-theoretic ideas to which functional, on which space?
choices: The Dirichlet energy on the loop space of a Riemannian manifold
The symplectic action functional on the free loop space of a symplectic manifold
The Euler characteristic on configuration space
questions.hint: Hamiltonian flow has periodic orbits; those should be the critical points.
questions.explain: Floer studied $\mathcal{A}_H(\gamma) = \int_{D^2}\bar u^*\omega - \int_0^1 H(t,\gamma(t))\,dt$ on the free loop space $\mathcal{L}M$. Critical points of $\mathcal{A}_H$ are exactly the 1-periodic orbits of $X_{H_t}$, recovering Hamiltonian dynamics as a variational problem.

questions.q: In Lagrangian Floer homology of two Lagrangians $L_0,L_1\subset M$, the chain complex is generated by...
questions.hint: Where does the action functional on paths from $L_0$ to $L_1$ have its critical points?
questions.explain: The action functional on paths $\gamma\colon [0,1]\to M$ with $\gamma(0)\in L_0,\gamma(1)\in L_1$ has critical points exactly at constant paths sitting at intersection points of $L_0$ and $L_1$. Generically the intersection is transverse and finite, giving a finite-rank chain complex.

questions.q: What does Hamiltonian Floer homology of a closed symplectic manifold $M$ compute (Arnold conjecture)?
choices: It recovers the singular homology $H_*(M;\mathbb{Z}/2)$ (or $\mathbb{Z}$ under suitable hypotheses).
It is unrelated to the topology of $M$.
questions.hint: The Arnold conjecture lower-bounds periodic orbit counts by Betti numbers.
questions.explain: Floer (and later Fukaya–Ono, Liu–Tian) proved $HF_*(M;H) \cong H_*(M)$ as an invariant. Comparing chain ranks gives the Arnold lower bound: the number of 1-periodic orbits of any non-degenerate $H$ is at least $\sum_k b_k(M)$ — the Morse-inequality analogue, in infinite dimensions.
